data.title: Now Is Not The Time To Be Bearish On The U.S. Economy
data.description:
Slow but steady

In 2025, economic growth in the US slowed modestly but remained resilient. Despite the largest tariff shock in a century, fiscal contraction, stock market crash and QT, the US economy still managed to grow around 2-3%. A perfectly average year.

Of course, the distribution of resilience among sectors and cohorts was not even. In particular, 2025 was relatively weak from a manufacturing and construction perspective. It was resilience in consumption and a lack of layoffs that helped keep the economy afloat.

For much of the past few years, manufacturing PMIs have been weaker than services PMIs. This was true again in 2025.

Having said that, Manufacturing PMIs have not been at recessionary levels when using a composite of the ISM and S&P data (a series that tends to have a higher correlation to actual hard data than either of the series on their own). Again, we have seen weakness in cyclical sectors, but not recession-level weakness.

Overall, most economic data in 2025 to date has come in at average levels. This is a trend my Economic Stress Index captures well. Right in the middle of the park.

Survey data on the other hand has been much weaker. This is particularly true of the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment survey. However, the NFIB’s Small Business Optimism Index has been consistently more positive, suggesting the weakness in the consumer surveys is attributable to tariff headlines and political uncertainty in addition to the widening gap between the rich and poor in the US, or K-Shaped economy, rather than actual recessionary conditions. Regardless, none of these data series have a particularly high correlation to actual underlying economic growth.

Looking now at key economic data at a more granular level, we can see further evidence that most of the headline hard data has been growing at average levels, with manufacturing data relatively weaker. As noted, employment and consumption have been much stronger. And, while both have slowed steadily in recent years, overall consumption is still coming in at average levels while employment growth is only slightly below. A lack of layoffs had also kept a lid on the unemployment rate, despite slowing job growth. The more volatile industrial production, retail trade and sales were relatively weak in 2023 and 2024 (but not at recessionary levels), and are now showing signs of modest recovery over the past quarter or two.

These more volatile data tend to be the cyclical drivers of the business cycle, and are more heavily impacted by borrowing costs and monetary policy. Despite their small overall share of the US economy, manufacturing, business investment, housing and construction are generally the cyclical divers of both the business cycle and employment cycle.

Cyclical data overall has been much weaker over the past few years than the much larger consumption and services side of the economy, but have not been weak enough to push the economy into recession. We can see below how manufacturing production and durable goods new orders spent much of 2023-2024 in contraction before showing signs of a recovery in 2025 (right as employment started to weaken). Private investment has also declined modestly but remains relatively strong.

More concerning has been the decline in construction spending and heavy truck sales, of which both data series are near recessionary levels.

The weakness in 2023, 2024 and the first half of 2025 in manufacturing and current weakness in construction has lead to a notable decline in cyclical jobs growth. However, manufacturing jobs growth appears to be bottoming as manufacturing activity is improving. The same cannot yet be said of construction employment.

The weakness in construction spending and job growth is largely being driven by a lack of activity in the housing market. For the past 18-24 months, the US housing market has effectively stalled as high mortgage rates and higher lending standards have crowded out buyers. Housing demand has declined as evidenced by weakness in new home sales, building permits and housing starts. Supply has risen and the appreciation in home prices slowed. Residential investment and housing construction activity have both contracted as a result, and thus the housing market has now become the primary drag on economic activity and employment.

On the services side of the economy, much of the resilience in consumption and overall economic growth can be attributed to the terrific shape of household balance sheets in aggregate. Debt levels and debt serviceability are at multi-decade lows, while cash balances and household net worth at all-time highs.

Overall, the US economy has been holding up well, though slowing at the margins. Job growth has been weak, but layoffs subdued. Manufacturing and construction activity have also been weak, but not at recessionary levels. We are seeing an improvement in manufacturing data, but not yet construction. Employment growth has been weak, but a lack of layoffs is keeping a lid on the unemployment rate. This economic resilience can be largely attributed to the following:

  • High income earners and the wealthiest Americans supporting consumption;

  • Multi-decade strength in household and corporate debt levels, debt serviceability, net wealth and overall balance sheets;

  • Persistent fiscal support (although this impulse weakened in 2025 thanks largely to higher tariff revenue);

  • Labour hoarding by businesses; and,

  • AI and on-shoring capital investment.

As a result of these factors, the US economy has been able to avoid recession and continue to growth at a resilient pace in the face of multi-decade highs in interest rates, inflation and tariffs, a housing market that has effectively stalled and weakness in the manufacturing sector.

The K-Economy

Much has been made of the K-Shaped US economy, so before we delve into the outlook for the US economy, it worth going over just how bifurcated things have become in the US between the have and have-nots.

Prior to COVID-19 and the extreme monetary and fiscal response to the crisis, the societal gap between income cohorts in the US was already stretched. It is now extreme. And, even though the wealth of the top 10% is supporting much of the overall US economic activity, the bottom 50% are struggling mightily with higher interest rates and lower wage growth.

The below chart illustrates the annual expenditures of US citizens by income quartile. The top 20% spend nearly as much as the bottom 80% combined.

Much of this is a result of the vast wealth the top 10% have accumulated. This wealth cohort makes up nearly 70% of total wealth in the US. These two charts go a long way to explain why consumption has been so strong over the past couple of years.

As a result, the bottom 50% are heavily reliant on debt to fund consumption and housing.

As such, higher interest rates have hurt borrowers (i.e. the bottom 50%), while it has increased interest income for those with little to no credit (i.e. the top 10%).

Home ownership in turn has been trending lower over the past couple of years.

While credit growth has also been contracting for years now as those who need credit simply can’t afford it.

The disparity in wage growth has also been stark. The bottom 25% of income earners have seen wage growth fall to its lowest levels in over a decade, while wage growth for the higher quartiles remains well above their respective pre-COVID trends.

And finally, these dynamics are only being exacerbated by a jobs market that is seeing little to no job growth outside of government and public service related sectors. There is little private sector demand for workers.

All of this is creating an economy that is almost entirely supported by the consumption of the wealthy, while tight monetary policy and high borrowing costs are constraining the majority of Americans.

Fortunately, we are unlikely to see consumption by the wealthy fall from here given the fantastic state of household balance sheets in aggregate (and because most of the spending is accounted for by the wealthy), while looser monetary policy will go a long way to assisting the less wealthy, as would a recovery in the manufacturing and construction sectors.

Lead indicators are improving

On a forward looking basis, there remains plenty of evidence to suggest the US economy will continue to prove robust, and is likely to accelerate in 2026.

Firstly, high frequency data shows Q4 real GDP growth will be around 2-2.5%, which is roughly average in recent history.

For Q4, the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow is suggesting GDP may have been as high as 5.4% on a real basis, highlighting just how far away from recession or any kind of material downturn the US economy has actually been. This number will likely come down as further Q4 data is released, but it shows just how well the US economy continues to do.

My composite lead indicator is also pointing to sideways growth over the next few months. This suggests economic robustness is likely to continue.

The Conference Board’s LEI also continues to accelerate off a low base, though it yet to move meaningfully higher. However, the correlation between the Conference Board LEI and actual economic growth has been minimal post-COVID.

As I touched on earlier, the cyclicality of the US economy is largely driven by a few sectors, notably manufacturing, investment and construction (particularly residential), much of which are intertwined. The trends in employment and consumption do matter, but even then, it tends to be employment within these cyclical sectors that drives fluctuations in overall employment, ditto with durable goods consumption driving volatility in overall consumption.

As a result, looking at leading data within these various sectors and areas of the economy provides great insight to where the US economy is headed over the medium term.

Beginning with the jobs market, much has been made of the weakness in jobs growth in recent times. But as we saw earlier, jobs growth is still at average levels, while cyclical jobs growth is contracting. This is largely a result of labour hoarding. That is, companies are not hiring new workers, but they are also not laying off existing workers.

Importantly, the outlook for the jobs market remains reasonable. Truflation’s Employment Index suggests that high frequency employment data has been improving over the past six months, which means officials jobs numbers should follow suit.

My employment leading indicator is also not pointing to further declines in cyclical or overall jobs growth, and is in fact pointing to acceleration.

In terms of consumption, there is also little reason to suggest we are likely to see consumers pull back their spending in the coming quarters.

Most important here is the wealth effect remains a tailwind to consumption and overall growth (even if it is largely being driven by the top 10%).

Financial conditions remain supportive of this dynamic.

As does CEO Confidence, though to a lesser extent.

The same can be said of household net worth, though the correlation between net worth and GDP growth has broken down somewhat post-COVID.

But, with asset prices rising and bank lending standards for consumer credit falling (inverted below), this is on net a tailwind for consumer spending.

High frequency retail sales data via the Redbook Sales Index continues to confirm consumer spending is robust and even accelerating.

The manufacturing, production and inventories cycle is where things get interesting.

As we saw earlier, durable goods orders and changes in durable goods inventories are highly cyclical and are the largest contributors the economic cyclicality outside of the housing market.

Firstly, until we see a proper recover in manufacturing PMIs, it is unlikely we see industrial production growth accelerate meaningfully. However, manufacturing PMIs are not suggesting industrial production is likely to decline from here as we close out 2025.

In terms of the outlook for the ISM manufacturing PMI, the ISM New Orders/Inventories spread is still only suggesting the PMI will move modestly higher in the short-term.

While the recent trends in the Regional Fed New Orders composite is pointing to a more favourable outlook for Manufacturing PMIs.

Easing bank lending standards for commercial and industrial loans is also a modest tailwind for industrial production over the next few months.

However, until we see the acceleration in a new inventory cycle, industrial production and manufacturing activity is likely to remain muted.

On that front, we are seeing positive signs.

ISM customer inventories are becoming increasingly depleted. This should translate into an increased level of new orders…

…and manufacturing inventories as a result.

The spread between the ISM manufacturer and customer inventories surveys are also suggesting a rise in durable goods inventories should commence in the coming months.

The resilience in consumption and retail sales in particular is also supportive of an imminent buildout in inventories.

As such, there is evidence to suggest a new cycle in inventory stockpiling could come about in 2026. We are already seeing signs of this via the improvement in manufacturing data, though the extent of any inventory buildout remains to be seen. Regardless, increasing inventory levels equals increased levels of manufacturing and production, both of which are strong cyclical tailwinds for the economy.

In terms of overall business investment and capital expenditures, it is still too early to call for a durable recovery.

While Regional Fed capex intentions are a volatile data series, this composite is not yet signaling a material pick-up in fixed investment. Concerns over tariffs are still likely having an impact on business spending.

Lending standards for small business also need to ease further from here before a durable recovery in overall investment can be called for.

Another big question mark for the US economy is the outlook for the housing market. As we saw earlier, housing activity has stalled over the past couple of years thanks to high mortgage rates crowding out potential buyers.

As a result, house prices have been largely flat over the past couple of years. This deceleration should stabilise over the next six months, as should the negative impulse in building permits and construction job growth. That said, the housing market is likely to continue be a drag on economic growth for a number of months still, and we need to see mortgage rates move lower to spur a durable recovery in the housing market.

Getting the housing market going again by lowering the policy rate is one effective tool the Federal Reserve has at its disposal to assist the bottom end of the K. As it stands, markets are only pricing in two rate cuts in 2026 and whether that is enough to spur a recover remains to be seen. Regardless, the economic impact from the housing market is unlikely to worsen from here and should improve on the margins over the coming quarters.

What we can be more certain of shifting from modest headwind to robust tailwind in 2026 is the fiscal impulse. While fiscal spending was obviously dovish this year, we did see a notable fiscal retrenchment thanks to increased tariff revenue. Now, with the Beautiful Bill set to come into effect in 2026, fiscal spending will once again become a material tailwind to the economy, with the CBO estimating a boost to growth by 0.9% in 2026.

2025 saw a tariff shock, QT, an equity market crash and fiscal retrenchment, and yet, growth is set to average around 2-3%. Now, with most of the negative impacts from tariffs behind us, a renewed fiscal impulse, improving manufacturing sector, improve leading economic indicators, a lower policy rate and QE, economic growth in 2026 could easily be 1-2% higher as a result. This is not a time to be bearish the US economy.

What is also constructive is that longer-term leading indicators also remain relatively supportive.

Oil prices tend to lead the business cycle by around 18 months and remain a tailwind.

So too the rate of change in interest rates.

Ditto the yield curve.

While my macro conditions index, which is a composite of the dollar, interest rates and commodity prices also remains largely supportive of the business cycle over the next nine months.

The only cause for concern from a long-leading indicator perspective has been the rise in ISM manufacturing prices. This is certainly something to keep an eye on, but for now the weight of evidence from long-lead indicators remains supportive of the US economy over the next 12-18 months.

Global growth also remains robust

What’s more, most leading indicators of the global business cycle are signaling a positive outlook.

Global monetary policy remains a tailwind for the business cycle as global central banks continue to ease on net.

While the diffusion index of OECD Composite Lead Indicators is also supportive of global growth.

This notion is supported by macro research firm Variant Perception, whose leading indicators for the US, China and Europe are all signaling positive outlooks.

Europe in particular is seeing constructive improvement in lead indicators. German manufacturing expectations are improving, Sweden’s manufacturing new orders are on the rise, while leading indicators of the OECD and Conference Board for Europe are showing signs of upside acceleration.

But, as has been the case for several years now, the biggest question to the global business cycle remains China. We did see signs of improvement from Chinese leading data in 2025, as evidence by the modest improvement in China’s credit impulse. But the supportive move in yields which tend to lead the credit impulse, M1 and PBOC balance sheet has reversed in recent months. Chinese lead indicators are also not showing encouraging signs of improvement.

While there is not yet enough evidence to suggest China’s economic struggles will worsen in 2026, particularly as we are likely to see further monetary easing, but the lack of a robust economic recovery in China may limit the duration of any recover in the global economy that appears likely in 2026.

Overall however, the weight of evidence continues to support a bullish outlook for the US economy and much of the global economy for 2026. In the US, the business cycle, earnings cycle, fiscal policy cycle and monetary policy cycle are all largely tailwinds. For risk assets this bodes well.

Summary, outlook and key macro notes:

  • The US economy has been holding up well, though slowing at the margins. Job growth has been weak, but layoffs subdued. Manufacturing and construction activity have also been weak, but not at recessionary levels. We are seeing an improvement in manufacturing data, but not yet construction. Employment growth has been weak, but a lack of layoffs is keeping a lid on the unemployment rate.

  • Due to multi-decade strength in household and corporate balance sheets, persistent fiscal support, re-shoring, AI investment and labour hoarding, the US economy has been able to avoid recession and continue to growth at a resilient pace in the face of multi-decade highs in interest rates, inflation and tariffs, a housing market that has effectively stalled and weakness in the manufacturing sector.

  • Trump 2.0’s tariff and DOGE policies have had little negative impact on the US economy, and look increasingly unlikely to do so as Trump pivots away from trade wars and fiscal austerity to more populist-type policies. Trump’s focus now appears to be tacking the affordability crisis and the K-Shaped economy, which bodes well for future fiscal spending.

  • Inflation has been rising and could continue to do so over the next couple of quarters. However, this is fully expected by investors and is unlikely to be Services driven inflation. Any inflation spike is likely to be temporary.

  • Most economic lead indicators continue to hold up and are not signaling any notable rise in unemployment, slowdown in growth or impending recession. The outlook for the manufacturing sector continues to improve, but the same cannot yet be said of the housing market. However, global liquidity growth appears to be peaking with lead indicators of liquidity also rolling over.

Right tail risks:

  • Trump wanted a US economy that is less reliant on government spending and is instead driven by the private sector. However, thanks to the bond market turmoil, mid-term election pressures and the checks and balances of the US political system, Trump appears to be pivoting away from his pro-tariff and deficit reduction policies and back toward more populist policies that got him elected in the first place. This likely means the negative impacts from tariffs and DOGE are likely to be much smaller than expected, while the “Big Beautiful Bill” is likely to accelerate the fiscal impulse in 2026, a supportive backdrop for the US economy. He now wants to grow his way out of the debt problem, which is a positive backdrop for risk taking over the medium-term.

  • The negative impact of tariffs is overstated, given how durable goods consumption is such a small component of GDP.

  • Lead indicators of the business cycle continue to suggest growth will be higher than consensus expectations over the medium-term.

  • Favourable net financing policy of the Treasury issuing bills of coupons should continue over the short to medium-term (though it is not a sustainable solution).

  • The Fed continues to favour an easing bias in the face of above-target inflation. Easier balance sheet policy to address funding stress is a positive for liquidity growth on the margins.

  • Overall, the “Big Beautiful Bill”, deregulation, tax cuts, potential upside surprises in economic growth, potential monetary easing and continued on-shoring are a supportive backdrop to risk taking over the medium-term.

  • Financial repression and dovish regime change at the Fed. The role of the central bank in period of fiscal largess and extreme high levels of federal debt is different to one of secular stagnation. Markets are telling us that 1) they understand we no longer live in an economy whereby inflation will average 2% and it is more likely to be around 3%, and 2) monetary policy is too tight. In a period of fiscal largess, a non-accommodative central bank results in the private sector being crowded out by the public sector and the K-shaped economy (as evidence by income disparity, lack of bank lending to SMEs, high delinquency rates for credit cards/auto loans, lack of job creation outside the public sector, stagnant housing market etc.). Populism wis likely to result in regime-change at the Fed to support the growing debt burden.

Left tail risks:

  • Structurally above target and acceleration inflation.

  • The Fed being unable (or unwilling) to sufficiently ease monetary policy, or worse, tighten monetary policy thanks to above target and accelerating inflation.

  • Funding issues in overnight repo markets as a result of the contraction of bank reserves. The Fed’s hawkish balance sheet policy is responsible for this dynamic, and, unless we see the TGA deplete by ~$300t or a resumption in QT, this dynamic will continue to drain liquidity from the system and lead to continue funding stress. A key risk here is if such funding stress leads to an unwinding of the hedge fund basis trade and blow-out in bond yields.

  • The repatriation of global capital out of US equity and bond markets back to domestic markets. The rise in European fiscal spending and re-militarisation, monetary policy nominalisation in Japan, dollar weaponisation and strategic decoupling from China are both legitimate dynamics that represent falling demand for US Treasuries and risk assets over the medium to long-term. A dovish-Fed may accelerate this trend. Not necessarily bearish for US equity markets, but could result in changes in relative leadership among global equity markets

  • A significant level of Federal debt is in need of refinancing in 2026 and 2027. If not met by a sufficient rise in global liquidity, then this could put notable upward pressure on interest rates, particularly if the Treasury is forced to transition to a net financing policy that is more skewed toward bonds over bills. A hawkish Fed will only exacerbate this potential left tail risk.

  • The erosion of Fed independence and the associated upside risk for bond yields.

data.title: Seagate Suffers From AI Success - Overbought With Premium Valuations
data.description:
I previously covered Micron Technology (MU) in December 2025, discussing how the hyperscaler demand for HBM3E/HBM4 had fully booked its 2026 capacity while driving strong pricing power, margin expansion, and supporting the company's multi-year growth thesis.

In this article, I shall discuss Seagate (STX) and share my findings about the stock, continuing the theme surrounding the storage and memory sector.

STX Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status

For reference, the current storage and memory sector comprises a few segments, including DRAM, NAND, and HDD, amongst others:

  • DRAM stands for "dynamic random-access memory, is the active memory used while operating a device to store code for algorithms, processes, etc," with some utilizing High Bandwidth Memory [HBM] technology for higher performance and lower latency,
  • NAND stands for "NOT-AND, is the memory used to store large amounts of data over time, such as documents and photos," with some utilizing solid state drives [SSD] technology for higher speed data access,
  • HDD stands for hard disk drive, similar to NAND in terms of usage albeit previously utilizing an "older storage technology—a spinning platter instead of memory cells," which explains the slower boot up time. There has been new technologies utilizing the next-generation heat-assisted magnetic recording [HAMR] to deliver ultra-high-capacity density as well.

For the uninitiated, STX is a company that provides data storage technology and infrastructure solutions based on HDD and SSD for numerous end markets, including storage subsystems for enterprises, cloud service providers, scale-out storage servers and original equipment manufacturers [OEM].

The last two segments are important indeed, given that the data center end market (including OEMs) comprise 75% of its FY2025 revenues, 80% of its FQ1'26 revenues, and ~77 of its FQ2'26 revenues (based on the data center revenues of $2.2B against the overall revenues of $2.83B).

As a result of STX's ability to participate in the ongoing AI boom, as observed in the robust overall revenue growth by +38.7% YoY in FY2025 and +21.3% YoY in H2'26, it is unsurprising that the stock has enjoyed an outsized 1Y rally by +259%, especially aided by the data center boom triggering the ongoing memory supply crunch.

Part of the optimism may be warranted after all, since the insatiable cloud/enterprise demand for large capacity hard drive storage has triggered the company's improved pricing power and operating leverage.

The same has been observed in the notably richer adj gross margins of 42.2% in FQ2'26 (+6.7 points YoY/+13.5 from FY2019 levels of 28.7%) and adj operating margins of 31.9% (+8.8 points YoY/+17.1 from FY2019 levels of 14.8%).

I am of the opinion that STX's ongoing "evolution of areal density" has been instrumental in driving its robust data center related monetization trends, thanks to the Mozaic 3+ HAMR technology platform's improved operating scale, the lower total cost of ownership by -25% per terabyte, the intensified storage capacity by +300% within the same footprint, and the lower power consumption by -60% per terabyte.

These developments underscore the robust demand for its Mozaic 3 HAMR drives at higher densities of 3 terabyte per disk at a quarterly shipment volume at over 1.5M units by the end of 2025, with the management already hinting at the ongoing qualification process for its second-generation Mozaic 4 terabyte per disk products through H2'26.

This is on top of their recent demonstration of "7 terabytes per disk capability in our labs," as the management remains focused on delivering their "long-term areal density road map that extends to 10 terabytes per disk, which we expect to deliver early in the next decade."

These developments naturally explain STX's nearly fully booked position in the calendar year 2026 along with their plans to "begin accepting orders for the first half of calendar year 2027 in the coming months" and their "cloud customers are discussing their demand growth projections for calendar '28, underscoring that supply assurance remains their highest priority."

This is on top of the management's disciplined supply strategy to drive top-line growth through "exabyte growth ... without increasing unit production volume," as observed in the data center shipment growth to 165 exabytes (+31% YoY).

Therefore, while STX's capex has been on the uptrend, it is merely attributed to the Mozaic 3 HAMR drives' ongoing production ramp up while remaining well within the management's "target of 4% to 6% of revenue" at 4.1% in FQ2'26 (+1.1 points YoY).

These reasons are also why the company has been able to report richer Free Cash Flow generation of $1.03B in H1'26 (+484.1% YoY) and a notably healthier balance sheet at a lower net debt position of $3.45B (+22.2% YoY/+31.4 from FY2022 peak net debts of $5.03B).

STX's Profitable Growth Trends Trigger Premium Valuations

For now, STX has further fed the exuberance with an outsized FQ3'26 revenue guidance of $2.9B (+2.8% QoQ/+34.2% YoY) and adj EPS guidance of $3.40 (+9.3% QoQ/+79.4% YoY), with the latter building upon the excellent H1'26 adj EPS growth by +57.7% YoY and FY2025 at +527.9% YoY.

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates appear to be rather promising, with the company expected to report accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +17%/+36.9% through FY2028, respectively.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of -2.8%/+10.4% and a 10Y CAGR of -4%/+5.9%, respectively, with it underscoring their renewed growth opportunities thanks to the multi-year cloud super cycle.

On the other hand, given the outsized stock price rally of +488.3% from the 52 week lows, STX appears to be rather inflated at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 30.38x, compared to the 1Y mean of 16.38x, the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 10.44x, and the sector median of 25.42x, despite the moderation from the 5Y mean of 58.39x.

My caution is attributed to STX's relatively lower HDD gross margins at ~50%, against the typically higher margin HBM/DRAM offerings at up to 70% in gross margins (for MU), albeit near to the NAND offerings at gross margins of ~45% (for NAND Flash product makers).

This development implies that STX's recent rally may have been overly done, as similarly observed in another HDD peer, Western Digital Corporation (WDC) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 30.49x, against the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 10.24x.

Otherwise, thanks to the consistently raised forward estimates, STX appears to be compellingly valued at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.91x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 30.38x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +33.2%.

A similar conclusion may also be observed when compared to the sector median FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.63x and STX's direct storage and memory peers, including:

  • DRAM and NAND memory - MU at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.16x (using the same methodology),
  • DRAM and NAND memory - Samsung (SSNLF) at 0.19x,
  • DRAM and NAND memory - SK hynix (HXSC.F) at 0.12x,
  • NAND memory - Sandisk (SNDK) at 0.38x, and
  • HDD - WDC at 0.78x, respectively,

with it underscoring their beneficiary status during the near-term memory supply crunch, for so long that the sector does not experience an "oversupply scenario in 2028-2029" attributed to the cyclical memory prices once "new fabrication facilities reach volume production" and/or the data center capex activities taper from current frenzied levels.

STX Is Initiated As A Hold Here

For now, STX has enjoyed a nearly vertical upward momentum since the April 2025 bottom, with the stock consistently chasing new highs away from the 100/200 day moving averages.

This reason is also why STX has been in the overbought territories over the past few months, as observed in the elevated RSIs and trading volumes, "as investors flock to these companies" and it remains to be seen how long the "memory party" may continue.

This is especially since the stock has already ran away from my bull-case fair value estimates of $346.90, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 30.38x and the annualized H1'26 adj EPS performance at $11.42 (+57.7% YoY).

While there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +56.2% to my bull-case long-term price target of $580.80, based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $19.12, it is also contingent on STX sustaining its expensive P/E valuations at ~30x moving forward.

This is a speculative conjecture in my opinion, attributed to the premium against:

  • the sector P/E median of ~25x,
  • the above memory peer group P/E mean at ~20x, and
  • the higher profit margin DRAM memory peer group P/E mean at ~9x,
  • along with the minimal margin of safety to my base-case long-term price target of $382.40 (based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $19.12 and the peer group P/E mean of ~20x).

STX's outsized rally of +259% over the past year and +418% over the past five years have also occurred faster than the last dividend hike of +2.8% and the five years' growth of +2.05%, resulting in the moderation in its forward dividend yields from 2020 levels of 5.03% to the current levels of 0.80%.

If anything, readers may want to note the recent bursting of the AI bubble in October/November 2025, with it remaining to be seen how long the memory sector's buying momentum may last as insiders also unlock some gains at current elevated levels.

With its era of quick gains behind us and the stock's overbought technical indicators/elevated short interest ratio of 6.02% implying near-term volatilities, I am of the opinion that it may be more prudent to initiate a Hold (Neutral) rating for the STX stock here.

Do not chase this rally over the cliff.

data.title: Hims & Hers: A Platform Strategy Pivot Driving Long-Term Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS), or Hims, is one of those stocks that never seem to stand still. Most of the attention gravitates toward whatever is most visible in the moment. A headline, GLP-1 sales numbers, a new regulation, or a product category that happens to be in or out of favor. Hims is liked or disliked depending on the latest development, often without much regard for how those developments fit together or where they are actually leading. Therefore, Hims is often viewed as a vendor for a series of products—for example, hair loss, weight management, or hormone therapy—instead of as a system.

I, however, believe viewing the company through that lens misses the more important question. The focus should not be on whether the most recent quarter was strong or weak or whether a specific product category will remain in favor next year, but on whether Hims is building the infrastructure layer for the future of healthcare. Contrary to common belief, Hims is not building a basic telehealth company. It is executing a cost-deflation platform strategy in healthcare, similar to the playbooks that powered Costco (COST) and Amazon (AMZN), and the introduction of "Labs" is the inflection point that elevates the company toward a structurally stronger business model.

In the sections that follow, I will look at Hims from a different angle by starting with where healthcare is likely headed and then working backwards to understand what Hims is building today and why I ultimately rate the stock a buy.

Starting From the End

Imagine a world in which healthcare no longer begins with symptoms. Instead of healthcare consisting of doctor visits due to sudden pain from largely avoidable but unaddressed conditions, the focus lies on managing risk early and deliberately. Health is managed continuously and almost invisibly in the background of everyday life. People no longer rely solely on how they feel. Instead, they have visibility into what is happening beneath the surface. Biomarkers replace intuition, longitudinal data replaces one-off checkups, and prevention replaces reaction.

This is not science fiction. It is simply the logical next step. So far these measures have not had a practical implementation into people’s lives, but the tools exist for this to happen. Every year millions of people are advised to follow repeatable steps such as getting a basic blood panel, checking cholesterol levels, and monitoring blood sugar to confirm there are no imminent health scares. These interventions usually need to be done once, or maybe twice a year. They are not invasive and are relatively cheap when compared to the issues that they can detect and prevent.

And yet, participation remains surprisingly low. Routine blood panels can be obtained widely across developed healthcare systems, but they are rarely used proactively. Many people only undergo testing when something already feels wrong or when a physician explicitly orders it after symptoms appear. However, the tools exist, and the required frequency is minimal, yet adoption remains limited. Why could this be? I would argue that availability alone is not enough.

Preventative healthcare as it currently exists is not ineffective due to being untried and untested. Instead, it fails to spread more widely as it struggles to integrate into real life. Knowing what is good for you does not automatically translate into action. Preventative steps can easily be deprioritized if they feel inconvenient or disconnected from everyday routines.

For preventive healthcare to work at scale, several conditions must be met at the same time. Measurement has to be simple. Access has to be frictionless. The experience must fit naturally into daily life rather than compete with it. And critically, the value must be obvious to the individual. Without resolving the above challenges, widespread preventative healthcare will remain compelling in principle but rarely adopted in practice.

The Missing Link: Making Preventative Medicine Practical

Hims' latest offering challenges this issue by embedding diagnostics directly into the platform users already engage with. On November 13, 2025, Hims introduced “Labs,” which provides 2 blood panels per year that measure up to 120 biomarkers and costs $349 as of January 2026. Through the Hims app, users can schedule a blood draw at one of 2,000 nationwide partner labs at a time that’s convenient to them. Once results flow back into the app, they are stored, tracked, and analyzed by medical doctors with the assistance of artificial intelligence. What was once an impractical event requiring multiple doctor visits in a fragmented system with time spent in waiting rooms has become an easy task where most of the heavy lifting is done by others.

Embedding diagnostics into the platform shifts the focus from requesting one-off medications toward continuous health optimization. By tracking biomarkers over time, users can view a dynamic personal health record that increases the likelihood of early detection for ailments and suggests the most effective targeted interventions. Beyond prevention, this data layer also enables personalized medication strategies for proactive health optimization like hormone balance, metabolic efficiency, or recovery.

From an investment perspective, this represents a structural milestone. Labs transforms Hims from a transactional telehealth provider into a vertically integrated healthcare platform. This creates recurring revenue streams and cross-sell optionality. A model that is scalable, repeatable, and inherently aligned with consumer demand for convenience.

But Labs itself is not intended to be the primary driver of incremental profit. When asked about the long-term margins of the lab's business, CEO Andrew Dudum was very explicit about future plans:

It’s going to go to zero. Anybody who is in the labs business today, whose core business is Labs, needs a new business. […] It’s in our best interest and it’s in our consumers’ best interest to verticalize this infrastructure over time and give it away for free. […] Because the data that you get back from it allows us to give you very tangible next steps and manage your care over 5, 10, 15 years, which is really where the long-term relationship and value creation comes from.

Clearly the goal is to control the primary consumer interface for healthcare by making diagnostics cheap, convenient, and deeply integrated into consumer habits. Once Hims has delivered value by providing critical health data at minimal cost and establishing long-term customer relationships, it can monetize over time through targeted, personalized products and medical service offerings.

Health as a Membership, Not a One-Time Transaction

Over the past decades, we have seen a common trend among some of the most successful businesses—they are built around memberships and recurring revenues instead of individual transactions. Costco does not optimize for margin per product, nor does Amazon Prime optimize for transaction-level profitability. Instead, both optimize for highest participation.

Customers pay upfront for access to a system that becomes more valuable the more they use it. This structure changes the economics entirely. Revenue becomes predictable, engagement increases, and churn declines. Most importantly, scale drives down unit costs, allowing savings to be passed back to members, which in turn attracts more members. The flywheel reinforces itself. Membership models work best when they deliver obvious everyday value through convenience, simplicity, and time savings. Healthcare, however, has historically been episodic, reactive, fragmented, and high-friction, making it a poor fit for a subscription model.

Now imagine healthcare re-engineered around those same principles: a membership built around simple biomarker tracking, low-cost access to critical health data, proactive medical guidance, and automated, personalized treatment pathways. The system becomes more valuable with every additional data point. Over time, longitudinal data builds a deeper, more precise understanding of individual health trajectories, enabling increasingly personalized and effective care.

To make this model economically viable at scale, Hims borrows directly from the Costco and Amazon Prime playbooks. As membership grows, unit costs per test decline. As costs fall, prices come down. Lower prices expand the addressable market. More members generate more data. More data improves interpretation and personalization. The panels become more valuable even as they become cheaper. This is the opposite of traditional healthcare economics. Instead of cost inflation driven by complexity and multiple layers of administration, it is cost deflation driven by scale and simplicity.

From an investment perspective, this is where the moat forms. Not through exclusive products. Not through branding alone. But through the combination of scale-driven cost advantages, accumulated insight, and growing customer trust. New entrants can raise capital, but they don’t have scale, and without scale, cost advantages never fully materialize.

Bringing It Back to the Present: Where Hims & Hers Fits Today

Hims has already created a diverse and established core business that offers standard prescription treatments—from hair loss to erectile dysfunction—with the simplicity of a few clicks. Over the past six years, the company has scaled from $83 million in annual revenue to over a $2 billion annualized run rate while growing its active subscriber base to 2.5 million members since its founding in 2017.

Everything is managed through the Hims or Hers app, a simple and intuitive interface used by millions of customers. However, it is important to note that the app is not a feature layered on top of the business; it is the business. Subscriptions, renewals, consultations, prescriptions, and now diagnostics all flow through the same channel. That matters because traditional healthcare is still deeply fragmented, and fragmentation is what causes a lot of inconvenience.

Once a trusted distribution layer exists, the question is no longer which specific product wins next. The question becomes who controls the interface through which those products reach consumers. Having aggressively expanded its user base and market share aggressively, Hims is positioning itself to become exactly that interface.

Management has been explicit about building toward this outcome, as seen in the CEO’s description of his ambitions:

But ultimately, my ambition, as we have done in the past is to bring these offerings to market, verticalize the infrastructure over time, which we've already started to do with our Trybe acquisition and other investments when it comes to lab diagnostics, establish that, systematize that, bring the cost down to incredible levels where you can really and truly undermine most of the peers on price and give customers huge access to this information and then ultimately be the trusted partner that can help them take that information and be incredibly action-oriented and precise with what the next step should be.

When viewed in the context of this statement, Hims begins to more closely resemble an infrastructure under development, rather than a provider of a diverse collection of supplement products. It is for this reason that short-term product debates often miss the larger picture.

Valuation: Paying a Fair Price for a Long Runway

For someone who usually fishes in classical value territory, valuing a company that is growing rapidly and still under-monetized is an uncomfortable exercise. But it is also unavoidable. Even the best business can turn into a poor investment if the price paid is too high.

Hims & Hers’ current market capitalization sits at roughly $6.6 billion, with shares trading around $29. Management has forecast at least $2.34 billion in revenue for FY25, thereby placing the stock at a price-to-sales ratio of about 2.8x. On a trailing-twelve-month basis, earnings per share sit around $0.60, which implies a P/E multiple of around 48.

Initially those numbers can send mixed signals, as the price-to-sales multiple looks reasonable for a company growing at Hims’ pace, whilst the price-to-earnings multiple looks high. However, this superficial perspective misses two important points, in my view. Firstly, it is rare that companies growing this fast are valued on current earnings alone. Typically they are granted higher multiples because today’s earnings understate future cash generation. For context, the revenue growth rates for the last three quarters were 111%, 73%, and 49%. Secondly, Hims is deliberately undermonetized. A significant portion of revenue is being reinvested into marketing efforts to expand the user base. Viewed through that lens, a P/E of 48x is not overly excessive—it can just as easily be interpreted as moderate.

Fortunately, management has given investors a concrete reference point. The company targets $6.5 billion in revenue and $1.3 billion in EBITDA by 2030. While I generally think EBITDA is a useless metric unless there is a sector-specific reason to use it, these figures are still useful as directional anchors. Historically, management has been conservative with guidance and tends to overdeliver, so I am comfortable taking these targets at face value.

Using those numbers, two simple valuation approaches converge on a similar outcome.

If Hims were to trade at the same price-to-sales multiple in 2030 as it does today, a $6.5 billion revenue base would imply a market capitalization of $18.2 billion. That corresponds to a stock price in the area of $81, translating into an internal rate of return (IRR) of approximately 22% from current levels.

Looking at the same outcome through a cash flow lens leads to a similar conclusion. Assuming normalization in margins toward a 15% free cash flow margin, Hims would generate close to $1 billion in annual free cash flow by 2030. Applying a P/FCF multiple of 20, which is not aggressive for a business with platform characteristics, yields a valuation of roughly $19.5 billion and a target share price of $85 with a ~24% IRR.

Neither assumption strikes me as overly ambitious, as maintaining the current sales multiple while executing on stated growth targets is plausible. Achieving free cash flow margins in the mid-teens once scale has increased is equally reasonable when you consider the operating leverage already apparent in the business.

Using the midpoint of these outcomes, I arrive at a 2030 target price of $83. Discounting this value back five years at a 10% rate implies a present fair value of around $52 per share, implying roughly 80% upside from current levels.

Taken together, this valuation case does not require multiple expansions. It requires continued execution along a path management has already laid out. That does not eliminate risk, but it does suggest that the long-term return profile remains attractive relative to the price being paid today.

Risks

Despite all of my previous arguments above, this does not disregard the fact that there are plenty of risks associated with this investment. Healthcare is an unforgiving industry, and it is rare that companies that try to change how it operates will do so unscathed. Instead of exploring the myriad possible downsides, I want to focus on the three material risks that, in my view, could genuinely impair Hims’ long-term strategy if they materialize.

The first key risk lies in execution, as Hims does not have an established playbook for building a consumer-facing healthcare platform at scale. Much of their strategy will rest on anticipation, quick execution, and judgment. It is positive that the CEO has significant skin in the game through a controlling ownership stake in the company. This provides the necessary operational flexibility to react to market conditions, but it also contains an element of risk. If major strategic decisions such as M&A or partnerships are poorly executed, it could be hugely detrimental to the investment case.

The next risk is competition. As outlined earlier, the moat around Hims has been forming for some time, but this does not mean they are impervious. Larger and better-capitalized players, whether insurers, Big Tech, or other healthcare providers, may decide that personalized healthcare is too important to ignore, and with superior resources, they could aggressively compete on pricing, marketing, or distribution before Hims establishes their market share.

The third and final risk relates to legal and regulatory challenges. While healthcare always carries legal and regulatory risk, these are particularly prevalent in areas such as telehealth, data handling, and compounded medical treatments. While Hims has preemptively invested heavily in compliance, it does not yet have the balance sheet or institutional resilience of a larger healthcare conglomerate, and so a significant regulatory setback or lawsuit could greatly slow progress or force costly modifications.

For me, the question is not whether Hims will face obstacles—it will—but whether the direction of travel remains intact despite them.

Conclusion: A Buy on the Bigger Picture

Hims & Hers is often debated through the lens of quarterly noise: product cycles, regulatory headlines, and short-term margin movements. Viewed that way, the stock will always look volatile and controversial. Viewed from the perspective of what is being built, it looks fundamentally different.

Rather than building a collection of medical treatments, Hims is executing a platform strategy to become the primary consumer interface for healthcare. The introduction of Labs marks a meaningful step in their strategy by turning inconsistent professional interaction inside a complicated and fragmented system into a regular relationship that will generate a better service at lower costs for customers.

This is not an easy company to value or to underwrite with precision, and their growth will not be linear. Execution, competition, and regulation will all have an important role. But for investors who believe healthcare is moving toward prevention and optimization rather than reaction, the asymmetric opportunity lies in owning the infrastructure before it becomes apparent to the market. For that reason, I rate Hims & Hers a Buy.

data.title: Alphabet Q4 Preview: Dark Horse Quantum Leader (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

After the Alphabet (GOOGL) was given a less than harsh sentencing to its Department of Justice case last fall, the stock has been soaring over the past several months, as well as year-to-date. The company has clearly cemented itself as a leader in the race to build the world's best LLM. Gemini continues to perform exceptionally well.

Shares are up more than 72% over the past six months, and they're also up more than 7% year-to-date. Over the last five years, shares are up ~270%, jumping from less than $100 per share to more than $300 a piece.

While it's clear Alphabet is a leader in AI, I actually wanted to focus on what comes next after AI: Quantum.

While not perfectly analogous, I think the next generation of Quantum computers will have an even greater impact on the future than AI has had over the last 3 years.

Alphabet is clearly putting together one of the most complete quantum computing stacks in the industry. For the company, this isn't about making headlines or grabbing attention. For Alphabet, their Quantum computing strategy is not a single invention.

With their massive search business behind them to subsidize their research, the company has a clear advantage, and it is built on years of success. The advantage is systemic.

Even after Alphabet's huge rally in 2025, the company is still valued only as a very mature platform. However, the market is barely crediting its top of the leaderboard leadership in quantum infrastructure, meaning that there is a major mispricing. I believe this creates a great opportunity for investors. Upside from quantum computing doesn't need to be immediate or even massive to matter. It simply needs to grow with Alphabet as it dominates its core businesses. The effects of having a powerful quantum ecosystem will take care of itself.

Heading into earnings, shares are upgraded from a buy to a strong buy.

Why I'm Doing Follow-Up Coverage

When I last wrote on Alphabet back in late May, I discussed the company's durability and ability to generate cash even in the face of the rise of AI agents. The reason for this follow up coverage isn't speculation, but it's more evidence of execution, which has me even more bullish than I was before on the company.

Recent findings (especially those centered around the company's Q3 earnings call) made it clear that Alphabet's efforts in the quantum field have paid off, and they have even crossed an important threshold.

On the Q3 earnings call back in late October, Alphabet CEO Sundar Pichai discussed the success of the Willow quantum chip.

Our Willow quantum chip achieved a major breakthrough, running an algorithm 13,000x faster than one of the world's best supercomputers," Pichai said.

And the result is verifiable, paving the way to future practical applications.

In my opinion, this is a critical shift in the narrative surrounding the company. Just six or seven years ago, Alphabet was seemingly only curious about quantum, and it was hardly a major player in the field. Now, the conversation has changed. The company is no longer just curious, but it is instead demonstrating that it can perform well.

This follow up coverage is meant to serve as a transition. Alphabet has officially moved from dabbling in quantum computing to now helping shape the direction of the industry. I think earnings next week will only add to this.

Assembling a Complete Quantum Stack

In order to fully understand Alphabet's quantum journey, we must rewind the clock back to 2019 with the Sycamore experiment. This demonstrated "quantum supremacy" by performing a task that was essentially impossible for even the most advanced supercomputers.

Fast forward to today and, as I've mentioned, the narrative has accelerated. With the major success of the Willow chip and related "quantum echoes" work, Alphabet says its quantum computing stack is 13,000x faster than a classic supercomputer, as Pichai stated in the Q3 earnings call.

This clear progression from simple curiosity to now an advantage over many classical computing competitors shows that Alphabet is learning how to repeat success.

From a physical standpoint, Alphabet literally operates a dedicated Quantum AI campus that integrates things such as fabrication, testing, and data-center-scaled infrastructure. The physical footprint accelerates these cycles in a way that hardly any competitors are able to match.

In a lot of ways, Alphabet's research is amounting to something akin to an arms race with other tech giants such as IBM. The faster Alphabet can develop a fault-tolerant quantum computer, the faster the internet giant can take a nearly undisputed lead in the lane they are trying to occupy in the future of quantum.

Q4 Earnings Preview

Heading into the earnings call next week, Alphabet is set to book another strong quarter of both top and bottom line results when the internet search giant reports on February 4th after the market close.

According to analyst estimates Alphabet is set to earn $2.64/share in EPS on revenues of $111.45 billion. If this pans out, this would represent 22.94% YoY growth in EPS and 15.53% YoY growth in revenue.

On the earnings revision front, Alphabet is coming from a position of strength. Over the last 3 months, there have been 34 upward EPS revisions and only 5 down revisions. On the revenue front, there have been 35 upward revenue revisions and only 3 down revisions.

On the earnings call, while I am sure it will be just a small reference, I will be looking closely for any updates related to their Willow quantum computing chip along with any key updates from quantum echos projects.

Alphabet has always been known to incubate "moonshot" bets years before they are financially viable. I believe their quantum computing bets will be no different.

Valuation

Alphabet has been on a tear over the last year and a half, yet I actually think the company still has more upside from here. Alphabet's valuation metrics continue to clock in well above the sector median (I think this is for a good reason).

Turning our attention to Alphabet's valuation metrics, we can see that the company's forward Non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio is currently at 31.86. When compared to the sector median of just 16.62, this is roughly an 91% premium. Seeking Alpha Quant gives the company a grade of a D on this metric.

Alphabet's forward Non-GAAP PEG ratio of 2.07 is also about 60% higher than the sector median of 1.29. Seeking Alpha Quant gives Alphabet a grade of a D+ on this metric.

Lastly, Alphabet currently has a forward EV/Sales ratio of 10.00, which is more than 390% higher than the sector median of 2.02. This metric is given a grade of an F, according to Seeking Alpha Quant.

Despite all of this, Alphabet actually I think has more upside from here simply on the promise that quantum can lift the company's long term P/E multiple.

For example, if we look at a pure play quantum company like IonQ, the quantum company trades at over 21 times forward sales based on its 2029 sales estimates.

By contrast, Alphabet trades at 6 times its 2029 sales estimates. While IonQ should trade at a higher price to sales multiple than Alphabet I think Alphabet has a real chance to radically change their business in 3 years from quantum. I think the power of Willow will be clear to investors and we will be able to articulate by this point how Willow will change the long run earnings potential of the company.

With this, I think shares should trade at roughly 8 times forward 2029 sales. This would unlock another 33% upside from here for Alphabet shares.

Risks

In my opinion, the bear thesis surrounding Alphabet and its ambitions to be dominant in quantum computing centers around timing. While they're a leader, timelines can certainly fluctuate. As the BBC reported, Alphabet's quantum computer is currently a world leader. That doesn't mean it will have a path to immediate monetization.

With this, revenue will remain immaterial for a while. Unlike other Quantum plays, like IonQ (IONQ), Alphabet is focused on generating revenue from their quantum computing unit because they already generate so much cashflow from other divisions. In essence, Alphabet, through their project Willow, is able to aim directly for a stable quantum computer vs. trying to find immediate product-market fit for what they have.

While I am by no means bearish on their quantum competitor IonQ, this does mean Alphabet can become a call option play for quantum if they're able to continue to improve their willow quantum supercomputing processor.

Alphabet's biggest risk around quantum is not the risk they bet big or bet wrong. It's actually the opposite. Ironically it's the risk they try to monetize it too soon and think too small.

Bottom Line

Perhaps the best thing that Alphabet has going for itself right now is that it's not racing ahead to declare victory in this quantum computing field. Instead, it is methodically positioning itself to control the industry and dominate its competitors when quantum actually matters to more people.

To me, Alphabet can reach this domination by owning the hardware roadmap, mastering error correction, attracting developers, and shaping standards. Alphabet has successfully completed this same strategy before through its Alphabet search ecosystem, and I know they are capable of doing it again.

Regardless of what happens in the future, Alphabet as a company has already positioned themselves extremely well to take advantage of this in the future. Investors should understand Alphabet's exceptional quantum positioning now, and they should not wait for quantum revenue to show up in the income statement, because by then, it may be too late.

With this, Alphabet shares are upgraded from a buy to a strong buy.

data.title: Celestica: A 2026 AI Opportunity With Growth Potential
data.description:
Celestica Inc. (CLS) is a hyperscaler-dependent manufacturer of Data Center components that are in high demand now that advancements in artificial intelligence have led to a massive CapEx splurge in the industry. The AI company just submitted a robust earnings scorecard for the fourth-quarter amid strong demand for its hardware products, including high-speed network switches, enterprise servers and storage systems. Celestica guided for more than $1.0B in CapEx this year, which will be funded entirely organically.

I like that Celestica is attaching itself to the long term growth plans of its hyperscaler customers and that the company is seeing enormous business momentum on all levels. I like Celestica's growth trajectory and initiate the AI player as a Buy.

Strong revenue growth, exploding core business

The hardware manufacturer benefited from strong demand for its hardware products in the Data Center industry, resulting in the report of $1.89 per-share in normalized earnings for the fourth quarter on Wednesday... which compared against a consensus estimate of $1.76 per-share. The revenue figure also beat expectations, by $166.5M, and came in at $3.65B.

Celestica is a hardware supplier for the Cloud computing industry. The company is seeing an explosion of demand right now for its high-speed networking switches, optical transceivers, high-bandwidth interconnects and AI-optimized servers... all components that are desperately needed as AI-driven hyperscalers ramp up their investments in server infrastructure.

In the fourth quarter, Celestica generated $3.65B in revenue from its hardware products that are consolidated in its Connectivity & Cloud Solutions (CCS) segment, showing 44% year-over-year growth. Celestica also widely exceeded its own Q4 '25 guidance of up to $3.58B in revenue, highlighting stronger-than-expected momentum in the company's core business.

CCS represented about 78% of the company's fourth quarter top line, with the remaining 22% falling into the ATS (advanced technology solutions) segment. This segment primarily deals with the provision of electronics products that are highly optimized. Products sold here include aerospace electronics, industrial automation systems and medical device subsystems.

The company benefited from soaring order volumes, and also managed to squeeze out some margin gains: Celestica reported a GAAP gross profit of $433.1M in Q4 '25, showing 46% year-over-year growth. In other words, the company’s gross profits, on a GAAP basis, are growing slightly faster than its top line, indicating positive operating leverage. The GAAP gross profit margin was 11.8%, showing 0.1 PP Y/Y growth.

Celestica is looking to grow its Data Center services aggressively in 2026 and the enterprise has guided for $1.0B in CapEx spending in 2026 in order to accelerate its growth strategy in accelerated computing. The outlook for the server industry is highly favorable for companies that provide critical hardware components: according to market intelligence firm Gartner, the IT industry is going to ramp up its CapEx spending this year, to an estimated $6.1 trillion... which implies a year-over-year growth rate of ~10%, with some sub-segments, like Data Center systems, expected to grow at double this rate in 2026.

Celestica is accelerating its capital spending in order to serve its hyperscaler customers like Alphabet (GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META), which are buying networking and high‑performance compute products from the supplier. As a result, Celestica has guided for an aggressive CapEx ramp this year which is expected to yield a 5-fold increase in its capital spending, from $200M in FY 2025 to $1.0B in FY 2026. The increase in CapEx is expected to be entirely self-funded.

Celestica’s valuation

AI start-ups are typically valued based on their potential for future revenue and earnings growth which in the case of Celestica is going through the roof. As the company’s growth is linked to the expansion plans of hyperscalers like Meta Platforms and Amazon's (AMZN) AWS, Celestica is riding a wave of elevated investment spending, driven by up-grades to the server infrastructure of U.S. Data Centers. This investment backdrop is supporting strong growth prospects and led Celestica to raise its revenue guidance for FY 2026 to $17.0B (+$1.0B).

Celestica is currently valued at a price-to-earnings ratio, on a FY 2027 forward basis, of 25.0X which compares to a P/E ratio of 23.3X for Broadcom -- which is also a hardware play on growing CapEx spending in the Data Center industry as well as custom chip production -- and 25.1X for Nvidia (NVDA)... the world's largest chip-design company and a major beneficiary of recent AI-driven hardware investments itself. Nvidia is also acquiring start-ups and is heavily investing in CoreWeave (CRWV), a preferred Nvidia partner for the allocation of new high-performance GPUs.

I believe that Celestica could trade at a ~30.0X P/E ratio longer term given its mission-critical product portfolio, high reliance on the CCS segment (which is driven by investments in the accelerating computing business) and growing margins in the core business. A 30.0X P/E ratio implies, using a forward FY 2027 consensus EPS estimate of $12.78, which calculates to a hypothetical stock price target of $383/share.

Risks with Celestica

The biggest risk for the Data Center supplier is a potential roll-back of AI spending plans if it turns out that artificial intelligence products and services cannot be monetized fast enough. Celestica is highly dependent on hyperscalers increasing their Data Center footprint, so a slowdown in spending would likely hurt the AI company fast and hard. What would change my mind on Celestica specifically is if the tech enterprise were to scale back its own CapEx guidance for FY 2026 as the year progresses, or if it suffered a gross margin contraction on the back of weaker CapEx trends.

Final thoughts

Celestica reported strong earnings for its fourth fiscal quarter, highlighting that the enterprise is a major beneficiary of the hyperscaler driving demand for AI-optimized hardware components in the Data Center industry. However, shares dropped 13% after earnings, likely because of the firm's aggressive CapEx guidance... which is going to weigh on cash flow.

I like that Celestica is focused on the hyperscaler market which consists of cashed up technology firms that are sitting at the forefront of the current AI revolution. I see a very favorable growth setup for Celestica given the firm's aggressive CapEx guidance (5X Y/Y) and see the post-earnings correction as a potential engagement opportunity.

While shares of Celestica are not cheap compared to other, more established Data Center plays, the company has the growth profile to sustain, or even expand its valuation, if hyperscalers can keep up their current spending levels throughout 2026.

data.title: Netflix: A Buy With Or Without Warner Bros. Discovery
data.description:
In early November, I reiterated my buy rating on Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) as I thought it was a golden opportunity to buy the dip in the stock. The fundamentals were robust and I concluded that the valuation contraction was an overreaction. As you can clearly see in the chart below, Netflix has not performed like I envisioned by any means. The company reported their latest results just over a week ago and so today we'll take a look at those numbers as well as some recent developments regarding their Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) deal.

Below, it is shown that Q4 was generally an impressive quarter for Netflix. While revenue guidance may be a little bit on the soft side, operating margin expansion is expected to continue. As for the Warner Bros. Discovery deal, there is potential for a meaningful amount of synergies but at this point there are regulatory concerns that could derail their intentions. That said, with the valuation being now near multiyear lows, I believe Netflix stock is a buy with or without the Warner Bros. Discovery deal going through.

Engagement & Slate

Let's start with a brief discussion on Netflix's engagement numbers and their slate. While the company did not provide specific numbers for Q4, it was shown that H2 2025 was generally stronger than H1. In H2, they reported that members watched a total of 96 billion hours on their platform, up 2% YoY. In the first half, there was a slightly lower 1% increase and so this indicates that overall engagement is growing stronger. This is an encouraging sign for the long term as at the end of the day, the amount of members and the amount of time they spend on the platform are indications of how much value Netflix brings to the table.

Furthermore, the company stated that their H2 strength was driven by their originals which saw viewings increase by 9%. Their Q4 branded slate included series like the final season of Stranger Things, and films like Frankenstein. Moving forward, their slate for 2026 is likely to drive continued engagement strength. For example, Bridgerton S4 and Avatar:The Last Airbender S2 are coming out as originals and so investors should monitor engagement figures for continued signs of momentum in the coming quarters.

Financial Results & Guidance

Revenues

In terms of their financial results, first things first, Netflix reported revenues of $12.051 billion and that represents a 17.6% YoY increase. This growth rate is really quite impressive as it is the strongest we have seen in the past five quarters. This indicates that the strong engagement and slate we discussed above seem to be getting translated in financial performance in a strong manner. Also, note that Netflix was able to beat analyst consensus by a pretty good margin of $83.91 million and so there was notable outperformance in the quarter.

Looking forward into Q1, the company has guided to 15.3% of top line growth and since that is a deceleration from Q4, that could be seen as a bit disappointing. Furthermore, while there was full-year revenue growth of 16% in 2025, they are expecting 2026 annual growth to come in at a midpoint of 13% and so while we saw good activity in H2 2025, that momentum is not expected to carry forward as strong as you'd like. Lastly, it should be mentioned that their ad revenue is progressing nicely and is expected to continue being a growth driver. This is an important signal as it shows that their strategy is working out and is paying off.

Profitability

In Q4, Netflix had an operating margin of 24.5% and that's a nice 230 basis point expansion YoY. Of course this is an indication that the company has managed their cost and expenses well and it perhaps shows that they have been resilient even when competition in this space is quite fierce. In Q1, they expect an operating margin of 32.1%, which would be a 40 basis point improvement YoY and so there are good signs that their corporate efficiency will remain strong. For full year 2026, they expect annual expansion in operating margin from 29.5% to 31.5% and so this is not an area that investors should place their concern in right now.

Now for the bottom line, Netflix posted a diluted EPS of $0.56 in Q4 and that's a 31% increase YoY. Firstly, this greatly outpaces top line growth to reflect the margin improvements stated above and it also represents an acceleration of growth from the previous quarter as another encouraging sign. In addition, they were able to beat expectations by a slim margin. As per their guidance, EPS growth is expected to drop back down to 15% for Q1 but at this point, with operating margin still projected to expand, I would say profitability of the business will remain solid in the near future.

Cash Flows Look Solid

Briefly, before I get into discussing their Warner Bros. Discovery deal, let's take a look at their cash flows. In Q4, operating cash flows were $2.112 billion, up 37% YoY and FCFs were $1.872 billion, up 36% YoY. Both of these increases are significantly stronger than both the top and bottom line growth rates to show that their cash generation ability has remained very strong.

While Netflix doesn't pay a dividend, they do buy back stock. Fully diluted shares outstanding dropped around 23 million shares QoQ to 4.317 billion and so they are doing significant share repurchases. To be specific, they bought back $2.08 billion versus $963.7 million in the year ago quarter and so management seems willing to share those strong cash flows with shareholders.

The Elephant In The Room

By now, it is no secret that Netflix has strong intentions to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery to strengthen their competitive positioning in the industry. In terms of the financials of the deal, Netflix is now proposing an all-cash deal at a share price of $27.75. As of this writing, Warner Bros. Discovery stock is at $27.60 and so the opportunity for merger arbitrage isn't particularly great at this point. Regarding this deal, in their Q4 earnings release they stated

First, Warner Bros.’ library, development and IP will allow us to provide an even broader and higher-quality selection of content for members; and, second, the addition of HBO Max will allow us to offer more personalized and flexible subscription options, better meeting the diverse preferences of our global audience.

To me, this sounds like there will be a significant expansion in their library and that there will be meaningful synergies as a result of this deal. Warner Bros. Discovery is trading at a forward P/E of 71.27 at its current price of $27.60 according to Seeking Alpha and so this deal is definitely not particularly cheap. However, in my view, the potential for these synergies to payoff likely makes Netflix's intentions make sense.

At this point, this deal is far from certain due to potential regulatory hurdles. On Jan 23rd, Federal Communications Commission chairman Brendan Carr stated that he had competition concerns while just two days ago (Jan 27th) some UK politicians have called for their competition regulator to conduct a full review of the deal. Lastly, just today (Jan 29th) it was reported that documentary filmmakers and independent movie theatres are pleading with state attorney generals to prevent the deal from happening. Overall, right now there seems to be significant competition concerns vis-a-vis the Warner Bros. Discovery deal and so even if it makes sense and is worth it for Netflix, there is potential it may not end up happening.

Valuation Contraction Overdone

Netflix's forward P/E ratio has seen a great deal of contraction since the high seen in mid 2025. At a current reading of 26.59, the multiple nears multiyear lows and so I believe there is a good amount of opportunity in the stock.

In today's analysis, it was shown that Q4 was a good quarter. Engagement was robust, revenue growth was the best we have seen in a while, and profitability was strong. Moving forward, there may be some deceleration in top line growth perhaps due to a potentially less attractive slate but margin expansion is expected to continue. Furthermore, their ads revenue is gaining momentum as a sign that their shift in strategy is working.

As for the Warner Bros. Discovery deal, as discussed above, it would be a nice addition to their business and synergies are likely but at this point, there is still great regulatory uncertainty and so I would't count on that deal happening. Nonetheless, with the forward P/E very near multiyear lows, I would say there seems to be too much pessimism in Netflix stock right now.

Conclusion

At this point, most investors' attention is probably firmly on the potential Warner Bros. Discovery deal. While I also believe that it could be a very meaningful addition to their business, the stock is a buy even if the deal were to fall apart due to regulatory scrutiny. Q4 was a robust quarter and while revenue guidance could have been better, overall FY2026 is still likely to be a resilient year. With that in mind, the forward P/E ratio being near multiyear lows suggests to me that investors are a bit too pessimistic regarding their prospects. Therefore, I'm reiterating my buy rating.

data.title: Geopolitical Environment Supports Oil As Weather Sends Gas Soaring
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy

Oil up for now but surplus set to weigh on prices

Oil prices have held up well so far this year, with several geopolitical events providing support to the market. The US arrest of Venezuela’s president and escalating tensions between the US and Iran pose risks to supply, particularly with the latter. An escalation between the US and Iran puts around 1.5m b/d of Iranian oil exports at risk – but clearly, any escalation would also raise concerns over broader Persian Gulf oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, where roughly 20m b/d moves through.
